title.value: Barriers and enabling factors for error analysis in NLG research
title.evidence: Barriers and enabling factors for error analysis in NLG research

authors.evidence: Emiel van Miltenburg, Tilburg University, The Netherlands. c.w.j.vanmiltenburg@tilburguniversity.edu∗   Miruna Clinciu, Edinburgh Centre for Robotics, Heriot-Watt University, University of Edinburgh,   UK   Ondrej Du sek, Charles University, Prague, Czechia ˇ   Dimitra Gkatzia, Edinburgh Napier University, UK   Stephanie Inglis, Arria NLG, Aberdeen, UK   Leo Leppanen, University of Helsinki, Finland ¨   Saad Mahamood, trivago N.V., Dusseldorf, Germany ¨   Stephanie Schoch, University of Virginia, USA   Craig Thomson, University of Aberdeen, UK   Luou Wen, Independent Researcher, UK

year.evidence: This position paper takes the stance that error analyses should be encouraged...

abstract.value: Earlier research has shown that few studies in Natural Language Generation (NLG) evaluate their system outputs using an error analysis, despite known limitations of automatic evaluation metrics and human ratings. This position paper takes the stance that error analyses should be encouraged, and discusses several ways to do so. This paper is based on our shared experience as authors as well as a survey we distributed as a means of public consultation. We provide an overview of existing barriers to carrying out error analyses, and propose changes to improve error reporting in the NLG literature.
abstract.evidence: Earlier research has shown that few studies in Natural Language Generation (NLG) evaluate their system outputs using an error analysis, despite known limitations of automatic evaluation metrics and human ratings. This position paper takes the stance that error analyses should be encouraged, and discusses several ways to do so. This paper is based on our shared experience as authors as well as a survey we distributed as a means of public consultation. We provide an overview of existing barriers to carrying out error analyses, and propose changes to improve error reporting in the NLG literature.

doi.notes: No DOI provided in the paper.

problem_statement.value: Despite the known limitations of automatic evaluation metrics and human ratings, few studies in NLG evaluate their system outputs using error analysis.
problem_statement.evidence: Despite known limitations of automatic evaluation metrics and human ratings, few studies in Natural Language Generation (NLG) evaluate their system outputs using an error analysis.

participants.evidence: We asked NLG researchers and practitioners for their opinions about error analysis, as well as factors that affect the likelihood of including one in their work.

value: Barriers and enabling factors to carry out error analyses
Necessity and usefulness of error analyses
questions.evidence: Participants were asked some general demographic questions, followed by questions about the following topics...

analysis_method.value: Quantitative and qualitative analysis of survey responses.
analysis_method.evidence: We performed a quantitative analysis of the responses to our closed questions. In addition, we performed a qualitative analysis of the open question responses...

barriers_identified.evidence: We identified nine different factors that might influence authors in their decision (not) to carry out an error analysis.
barriers_identified.notes: These barriers were highlighted based on survey responses.

enabling_factors.evidence: Participants recognized all the identified factors act as barriers to completing error analyses.
enabling_factors.notes: These factors could facilitate the adoption of error analyses.

participant_feedback.value: Participants generally appreciate error analyses they see in the work of others but face barriers to conducting them themselves.
participant_feedback.evidence: Our findings suggest that NLG researchers generally appreciate error analyses they see in the work of others, but they are held back from carrying out an error analysis themselves for various reasons.
participant_feedback.notes: Feedback reflects a positive sentiment towards error analysis.

cultural_incentives.value: A culture change towards error analysis is necessary to encourage its adoption in NLG.
cultural_incentives.evidence: Most participants thought a culture change is necessary to make error analysis a common practice.
cultural_incentives.notes: Cultural factors play a significant role in the adoption of error analysis.

value: Develop tools and resources for error analysis
Encourage structural changes in research publication incentives
future_directions.evidence: In the future, we would like to focus on developing tools and resources, such as error taxonomies, annotation tools, and clear guidelines...
future_directions.notes: Future work aims to enhance the practice of error analysis.

data_access.value: Data and code are freely available online.
data_access.evidence: The code and data for this research project are freely available online.

code_access.evidence: We made sure to analyse our responses before the deployment of our survey, and committed the report to GitHub.
